title: Exothermic and endothermic reactions — reactions that release or absorb heat
examTip: For exothermic reactions, ΔH is NEGATIVE and temperature rises. For endothermic, ΔH is POSITIVE and temperature falls. On an energy profile, the activation energy is always the hump above the reactants — not the overall energy change.
introTips: Remember: exo = exit — energy exits the reaction into the surroundings. Endo = enter — energy enters the reaction from the surroundings.
The sign of ΔH tells you everything: negative = exothermic, positive = endothermic.
Catalysts lower activation energy but do NOT change the overall energy change (ΔH).
intro: Every reaction either warms the world around it or cools it down. Hand warmers heat up, cold packs in a first aid kit cool down — both are chemistry at work. The difference comes down to whether the reaction releases or absorbs energy. This isn't just theory: energy changes in reactions power everything from your food to the fuel in an aeroplane. Understanding whether a reaction is exothermic or endothermic — and why — is central to chemistry and to the exam.

keyPoints.heading: Exothermic reactions — releasing energy to the surroundings
keyPoints.content:
An exothermic reaction releases energy — usually as heat — to the surroundings. You can tell because the temperature of the surroundings INCREASES. The products have less energy stored in them than the reactants — the difference is released as heat. ΔH is negative for exothermic reactions (energy leaves the system).

Examples of exothermic reactions:
• Combustion — burning any fuel (methane, wood, petrol) releases heat
• Neutralisation — acid + alkali → salt + water; temperature rises
• Respiration — glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water (in your cells)
• Oxidation of metals — rusting (slow, releases heat gradually); hand warmers (iron oxidation)
• Explosions — rapid combustion releasing enormous amounts of heat and light

On an energy profile diagram: the reactants are at a higher energy level than the products. The energy difference between reactants and products = energy released = |ΔH|.
writeBullets: Exothermic reaction — releases energy (heat) to the surroundings; temperature of surroundings increases
Products have LESS energy than reactants — the difference is released
ΔH is negative for exothermic reactions
Examples: combustion, neutralisation, respiration, oxidation of metals, hand warmers
Energy profile diagram: reactants higher than products; arrow points downward from reactants to products

keyTerms.def: A reaction that releases energy to the surroundings, causing the temperature of the surroundings to rise; ΔH is negative
Enthalpy change — the energy transferred to or from the surroundings during a reaction. Negative = exothermic, positive = endothermic

keyPoints.memTip: Exo = exit. Energy EXits the reaction and goes into the surroundings — they warm up.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse 'the reaction gets hot' with 'the reactants heat up'. It's the SURROUNDINGS that warm — the reaction itself is releasing energy.

keyPoints.heading: Endothermic reactions — absorbing energy from the surroundings
keyPoints.content:
An endothermic reaction absorbs energy from the surroundings. The temperature of the surroundings DECREASES because the reaction is taking heat in. The products have MORE energy stored in them than the reactants — energy has been absorbed and stored. ΔH is positive for endothermic reactions.

Examples of endothermic reactions:
• Thermal decomposition — CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (requires constant heating)
• Photosynthesis — absorbs light energy from the sun
• Dissolving ammonium nitrate in water — used in cold packs for sports injuries; the pack feels cold because heat is absorbed from your hand
• Electrolysis — requires electrical energy input to decompose substances
• Citric acid + sodium hydrogencarbonate — temperature drops (common school practical)

On an energy profile diagram: the products are at a HIGHER energy level than the reactants.
writeBullets: Endothermic reaction — absorbs energy from the surroundings; temperature of surroundings decreases
Products have MORE energy than reactants — energy has been absorbed and stored
ΔH is positive for endothermic reactions
Examples: thermal decomposition, photosynthesis, dissolving ammonium nitrate, electrolysis
Energy profile diagram: products higher than reactants; arrow points upward from reactants to products
Cold packs work because dissolving ammonium nitrate absorbs heat from the surroundings

keyTerms.def: A reaction that absorbs energy from the surroundings, causing the temperature of the surroundings to fall; ΔH is positive
The breakdown of a compound into simpler substances using heat — an endothermic process

keyPoints.memTip: Endo = enter. Energy ENters the reaction FROM the surroundings — the surroundings cool down.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Cold packs are a great real-world hook. Students find 'the solution feels cold' intuitively correct — the reaction is absorbing heat from your hand.

keyPoints.heading: Activation energy and energy profile diagrams
keyPoints.content:
Activation energy is the minimum energy that reactant particles must have to collide successfully and react. Every reaction — exothermic OR endothermic — requires an activation energy to get started. On an energy profile diagram, activation energy appears as the 'hump' or peak above the reactants.

Key features of energy profile diagrams:
• Reactants: energy level on the left
• Transition state: the peak (highest point) — the activation energy
• Products: energy level on the right
• For exothermic: peak is above reactants; products are lower than reactants
• For endothermic: peak is above reactants; products are higher than reactants

Catalysts provide an alternative reaction pathway with a LOWER activation energy. The same products are formed, and ΔH is unchanged — but more particles have enough energy to react, so the reaction is faster. On an energy profile diagram, a catalyst is shown as a lower peak.

Why some exothermic reactions need a spark: Methane doesn't spontaneously combust in air because the activation energy is high — a spark or flame provides the initial energy needed to start the reaction.
writeBullets: Activation energy — minimum energy needed for reactant particles to react successfully
Shown as the 'hump' above the reactants on an energy profile diagram
Large activation energy → reaction is slow or needs heating/spark to start
Catalyst: provides alternative pathway with lower activation energy → faster reaction
Catalyst does NOT change ΔH or the reactants/products — only lowers the activation energy
Lower activation energy means more particles have enough energy to react → greater rate

keyTerms.def: The minimum energy required for a reaction to occur — the energy needed to break bonds in the reactants to start the reaction
A substance that increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy; not used up in the reaction
A graph showing the energy of reactants, transition state (peak), and products during a reaction

keyPoints.memTip: Activation energy = the energy 'hill' you have to climb before the reaction can run downhill. Catalysts make the hill smaller.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students frequently draw catalysts as changing ΔH — emphasise that the start and end energy levels don't change, only the peak height.

keyPoints.heading: Practical — measuring temperature changes and calculating energy
keyPoints.content:
You can measure whether a reaction is exothermic or endothermic by monitoring temperature change.

Method:
1. Measure the temperature of the reactant(s) in an insulated container (polystyrene cup with lid reduces heat loss).
2. Add the second reactant and stir.
3. Record the maximum (exothermic) or minimum (endothermic) temperature reached.
4. Calculate ΔT = T_final − T_initial. Positive ΔT = exothermic. Negative ΔT = endothermic.

Calculating energy transferred: Q = mcΔT
Q = energy in joules (J)
m = mass of solution in grams (g) — usually assumed to be the volume in cm³ if density ≈ 1 g/cm³
c = specific heat capacity of water = 4.18 J/(g·°C)
ΔT = temperature change in °C

Worked example: 100 g of water warms by 5°C in a neutralisation reaction.
Q = 100 × 4.18 × 5 = 2090 J = 2.09 kJ.

Sources of error: heat loss to surroundings (use insulated cup and lid), inaccurate thermometer, not stirring — all reduce the measured temperature change.
writeBullets: ΔT = T_final − T_initial; positive = exothermic, negative = endothermic
Q = mcΔT where m = mass (g), c = 4.18 J/g°C (water), ΔT = temperature change
Polystyrene cup reduces heat loss; lid reduces evaporation errors
Mass of solution ≈ volume in cm³ when density ≈ 1 g/cm³ (dilute aqueous solutions)

keyTerms.def: Equation for energy transferred: Q in joules, m in grams, c = 4.18 J/g°C for water, ΔT in °C
The energy needed to raise 1 g of a substance by 1°C; for water c = 4.18 J/g°C
Temperature change = T_final − T_initial; positive means temperature rose (exothermic)

keyPoints.memTip: Q = mcΔT. 'm' is the mass of solution, 'c' is always 4.18 for water in GCSE, 'ΔT' is how much the temperature changed.
keyPoints.tutor_note: In the exam, always use c = 4.18 J/g°C unless a different value is given. Also remind students to convert kJ to J (× 1000) or J to kJ (÷ 1000) carefully.

tables.title: Exothermic vs endothermic — comparison
rows: Cold packs (sports injuries), cooking (energy input needed)

0.question: The temperature of a solution drops from 22°C to 16°C when two reagents are mixed. What type of reaction is this?
options: Endothermic — the reaction absorbs heat from the surroundings
Exothermic — the reaction releases heat to the surroundings
Exothermic — the temperature decreased so energy was released
Endothermic — the products have less energy than the reactants
0.correctFeedback: Correct! The temperature DECREASED — the surroundings lost heat to the reaction. This is endothermic. ΔH is positive.
0.wrongFeedback: The temperature decreased — the surroundings are getting colder because the reaction is absorbing heat from them. This is endothermic. ΔH is positive.

2.question: What does a catalyst do to the activation energy and the overall energy change (ΔH) of a reaction?
options: Lowers both activation energy and ΔH
2.correctFeedback: Exactly right! A catalyst lowers activation energy by providing an alternative pathway — but ΔH (the overall energy change) is not affected.
2.wrongFeedback: A catalyst lowers activation energy only. ΔH stays the same because the reactants and products are identical — only the route to get there changes.

3.question: Using Q = mcΔT: 50 g of solution warms by 8°C. How much energy is released? (c = 4.18 J/g°C)
3.wrongFeedback: Use Q = mcΔT = 50 × 4.18 × 8 = 1672 J. Multiply mass × specific heat capacity × temperature change.

commonMistakes: Saying 'an exothermic reaction heats up the reactants' — it is the SURROUNDINGS that heat up, not the reactants themselves.
Confusing the sign of ΔH — exothermic is NEGATIVE (energy leaves the reaction), endothermic is POSITIVE (energy enters the reaction).
Drawing a catalyst as changing ΔH on an energy profile diagram — catalysts only lower the activation energy (the peak), not the overall energy difference.
Using the wrong mass in Q = mcΔT — always use the total mass of the solution in grams, not the volume in cm³ (though they're similar for dilute solutions).
Labelling the activation energy as the full height from the x-axis to the peak, rather than from the reactants' energy level to the peak.
examTips: On energy profile diagrams, label all three key features: reactants' energy, products' energy, and the activation energy (the peak). Show the arrow for ΔH between reactants and products — not from zero.
For Q = mcΔT questions, always show the substitution step: Q = (mass) × 4.18 × (ΔT). You get marks for the method even if arithmetic goes wrong.
If asked to compare a catalysed and uncatalysed energy profile, draw two peaks — the catalysed peak is lower — but reactants and products stay at the same level.
In 'explain why the temperature decreased' questions, say: 'the reaction is endothermic and absorbed heat energy from the surroundings, so the temperature of the surroundings fell'.
revisionCardBullets: Exothermic: releases energy to surroundings; temperature rises; ΔH negative
Endothermic: absorbs energy from surroundings; temperature falls; ΔH positive
Exo examples: combustion, neutralisation, respiration, oxidation of metals
Endo examples: thermal decomposition, photosynthesis, dissolving NH₄NO₃
Energy profile: exo — products lower than reactants
Energy profile: endo — products higher than reactants
Activation energy: energy 'hump' above the reactants — minimum energy to react
Catalyst: lowers activation energy, provides alternative pathway, ΔH unchanged
Q = mcΔT: m in g, c = 4.18 J/g°C, ΔT in °C
ΔT positive = exothermic; ΔT negative = endothermic
Polystyrene cup reduces heat loss in temperature change practicals
ΔH sign: negative = exothermic; positive = endothermic
